Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Viola spp.; Campanula spp.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in carnation, rose, and chrysanthemum; flower color/metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms delphinidin branch precursors for target anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key gene to engineer blue/blue-violet flowers in species lacking delphinidin biosynthesis; may need stacking with suitable DFR and low endogenous F3'H background
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Introduction of F3'5'H from heterologous species enabled delphinidin-based anthocyanin production in ornamental hosts lacking this activity. 2. This shifted flower colour toward blue-purple. 3. Engineering success depended on competition with endogenous F3'H and compatibility with host DFR.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Color outcome may be limited by endogenous F3'H activity and host DFR substrate specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Campanula F3'5'H was expressed under chrysanthemum F3H promoter in engineered chrysanthemums.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the flavonoid B-ring at the 3' and 5' positions to produce precursors of delphinidin-derived anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Dianthus caryophyllus; Rosa spp.; Chrysanthemum morifolium
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of delphinidin-based anthocyanins and blue/blue-violet flower colour.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Replacement of endogenous host DFR in transgenic flowers; substrate preference evidence; color/metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful companion gene with F3'5'H when host DFR poorly accepts trihydroxylated substrates; improves delphinidin-based pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Host DFR specificity can restrict production of blue delphinidin-derived pigments after F3'5'H introduction. 2. Petunia or iris DFR better accepts trihydroxylated dihydromyricetin. 3. Replacing endogenous DFR can enhance blue-ish flower coloration.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires transgenic replacement/stacking; color still depends on decoration and competing enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted as necessary in some host species after heterologous F3'5'H introduction.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols such as dihydromyricetin to the corresponding leucoanthocyanidins.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Dianthus caryophyllus; Rosa spp.; Chrysanthemum morifolium
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables/improves delphinidin-derived anthocyanin accumulation in hosts with unsuitable endogenous DFR.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Natural/engineered low-activity background used in chrysanthemum transformation strategy
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing endogenous F3'H can increase flux toward delphinidin when F3'5'H is introduced
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Endogenous F3'H competes with introduced F3'5'H in chrysanthemum. 2. Low F3'H activity backgrounds were selected or engineered to enhance delphinidin production. 3. This improved blue/violet coloration.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Cyanidin-based anthocyanins; indirectly affects delphinidin-based anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing F3'H may alter native pigment composition.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a competitive branch enzyme directly relevant to engineering outcome in chrysanthemum.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the flavonoid B-ring at the 3' position, channeling flux toward cyanidin derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Competes against delphinidin engineering; reduced activity favors bluer flowers.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inhibition of acylating activity in engineered gentian; flower color phenotype
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target to shift flower color from blue toward red-purple by reducing aromatic diacylation and intramolecular co-pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In gentian, inhibiting a 5,3'-acyltransferase reduced diacylated delphinidin anthocyanins. 2. Reduced intramolecular co-pigmentation. 3. Flowers became more red-purple.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced pigment stability/blue hue may accompany reduced acylation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Exact gene symbol not provided in article text.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers acyl groups to glycosylated anthocyanins at the 5 and 3' positions, generating diacylated anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Its inhibition reduces diacylated anthocyanins and shifts flowers to red-purple.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inhibition/silencing in lisianthus with flower color assessment
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Downregulation can reduce flavonol copigments and make flowers redder; useful for tuning anthocyanin display
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Inhibition of FLS in lisianthus reduced flavonol production. 2. This decreased intermolecular co-pigmentation of anthocyanins by flavonols. 3. Flowers appeared redder.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May reduce flavonol-associated functions while altering color.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts indirectly on anthocyanin visual phenotype through copigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts dihydroflavonols to flavonols, diverting flux from anthocyanin synthesis and producing copigments.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Inhibition makes flowers redder by reducing flavonol-mediated intermolecular copigmentation.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Anthocyanidin reductase, reductase involved in proanthocyanidin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inhibition in soybean seed coat with seed color phenotype and anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Silencing/editing can generate red anthocyanin-rich seed coats and visual markers for transgenic seeds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANR normally removes anthocyanidins to supply epicatechin for proanthocyanidin synthesis. 2. Inhibition of ANR in soybean seed coat led to accumulation of red cyanidin-based anthocyanins. 3. The resulting red seed color was proposed as a transgenic marker.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter proanthocyanidin content and seed coat chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes visual marker application of the engineered phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to flavan-3-ols such as epicatechin for proanthocyanidin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Inhibition redirects flux from proanthocyanidins to red cyanidin-based anthocyanin accumulation in seed coat.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression in tomato fruit and tobacco/tomato cell cultures with anthocyanin metabolite profiling and visible pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful regulator to induce high anthocyanin accumulation when combined with Delila; useful for nutritional enhancement, natural colorant production, and visual markers
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Tomato E8 promoter (fruit-specific); CaMV 35S promoter (cell culture)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in MBW complex with bHLH partner Delila and endogenous WDR proteins
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major specialized MYB determinant limiting anthocyanin accumulation in specific tissues; key activator for high-level anthocyanin engineering
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates anthocyanin biosynthetic genes across the pathway and transport-associated genes when co-expressed with Delila
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rosea1 combined with Delila induced very high anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit, reaching about 3% dry weight. 2. Fruit-specific E8-driven expression avoided yield penalty by activating the pathway late in ripening. 3. The engineered purple tomatoes showed extended shelf life and reduced susceptibility to Botrytis cinerea, and were effective in a mouse dietary intervention.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Little to no yield penalty when expressed late in fruit development; ectopic expression could have broader effects.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant activity; health-promoting dietary compound; improves shelf life and disease tolerance
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic genes; transporters and transport-associated proteins
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Breaker-stage tomato fruit under E8 promoter; transformed tobacco/tomato suspension cells under 35S promoter
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Fruit ripening stage via E8 promoter in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Postharvest pathogen challenge; late fruit ripening context
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Monocot/dicot MYB compatibility issues are discussed generally, but Rosea1 works efficiently in dicot hosts.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly promotes anthocyanin accumulation, producing purple fruit/cells with added nutritional and postharvest benefits.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression with Rosea1 in tomato fruit and tobacco/tomato cell cultures; pigmentation and anthocyanin profiling
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Effective co-regulator with MYB factors for maximizing anthocyanin production in crops and cell cultures
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms MBW complex with Rosea1 and endogenous WDR proteins
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Enhances induction strength when co-expressed with MYB activator; supports maximal anthocyanin production
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Co-activates anthocyanin biosynthetic and transport genes with Rosea1
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Delila, together with Rosea1, enabled high anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit and cultured cells. 2. Co-expression produced levels comparable to dark berries and stable high-producing cell cultures. 3. In tomato fruit, this contributed to purple coloration, extended shelf life, and reduced Botrytis susceptibility.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: bHLH proteins often have broader developmental roles, but fruit-specific expression mitigated penalties in tomato.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant activity; nutritional enhancement; postharvest protection
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic genes; transporters and transport-associated proteins
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Breaker-stage tomato fruit under E8 promoter; transformed suspension cells under 35S promoter
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Fruit ripening stage via E8 promoter in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Postharvest pathogen challenge; late fruit ripening context
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes that MYB is often limiting, but combined MYB+bHLH expression achieves maximum induction.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Co-expression with Rosea1 strongly increases anthocyanin accumulation and purple pigmentation.
